gemma4-it:e4b.input: make prompt for claude: build a windows hotkey app that fixes grammar with local llm only, no cloud, include tests and installer notes
gemma4-it:e4b.output:
<task>Develop a complete specification and implementation plan for a Windows hotkey application that performs real-time grammar correction using only a locally hosted LLM.</task>
<context>The application must function as a system-wide hotkey listener, intercepting text input to apply grammar fixes. All processing, including the LLM inference, must occur entirely on the user's local machine without external cloud API calls.</context>
<constraints>The plan must include detailed unit and integration tests for core functionality (hotkey capture, LLM invocation success/failure). It must also provide clear installation instructions suitable for a non-technical end-user. The tone should be highly technical and prescriptive.</constraints>
<output_format>Markdown document with the following structure: `# Project Overview`, `## Technical Specification`, `### Local LLM Integration Details`, `## Testing Strategy (Unit/Integration)`, `## Installation & Usage Notes`</output_format>

gemma4-it:e4b.input: prompt: debug why flm serve is slow at 32k context, compare gemma nanbeige qwen, output a table and recommendation
gemma4-it:e4b.output:
<task>Debug the performance bottleneck of `flm serve` when operating with a 32k context window and compare its speed against Gemma, Nanbeige, and Qwen.</task>
<context>The user is experiencing slow response times from `flm serve` specifically when using a 32k context size. The comparison should involve three specific models: Gemma, Nanbeige, and Qwen.</context>
<constraints>Output must include a comparative table detailing performance metrics (e.g., latency/throughput) for all four models under the specified conditions. The tone must be technical and analytical.</constraints>
<output_format>Markdown table followed by a concise recommendation section.</output_format>
